The **Kronos Quartet** is an American [string quartet](/wiki/String_quartet "String quartet") based in San Francisco.[[1]](#cite_note-1) It has been in existence with a rotating membership of musicians for 50 years. The quartet covers a very broad range of musical genres, including [contemporary classical music](/wiki/Contemporary_classical_music "Contemporary classical music"). More than 1,200 works have been [written for it](/wiki/Commission_(art) "Commission (art)"). The quartet has recorded over 75 albums and received a number of awards.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kronos_Quartet&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The quartet was founded by violinist David Harrington in [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"), Washington. Its first performance was in November 1973.[[2]](#cite_note-Graves-2) Since 1978, the quartet has been based in San Francisco, California. It is named after the Greek Titan [Kronos](/wiki/Cronus "Cronus"). The longest-running combination of performers (from 1978 to 1999) had Harrington and John Sherba on violin, Hank Dutt on viola, and [Joan Jeanrenaud](/wiki/Joan_Jeanrenaud "Joan Jeanrenaud") on cello. In 1999, Jeanrenaud left Kronos because she was "eager for something new";[[3]](#cite_note-Templeton-3) she was replaced by Jennifer Culp,[[4]](#cite_note-notes-4) who, in turn, left in 2005 and was replaced by Jeffrey Zeigler. In June 2013, Zeigler was replaced by Sunny Yang.[[5]](#cite_note-press1-5) In February 2023, cellist and composer [Paul Wiancko](/wiki/Paul_Wiancko "Paul Wiancko") became the quartet's newest cellist.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In March 2024, Kronos Quartet announced that Sherba and Dutt would retire in June of that year. They were replaced by violinist Gabriela Díaz and violist Ayane Kosaza.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

With over 75 studio albums to its credit and having performed worldwide, the Kronos Quartet has been called "probably the most famous '[new music](/wiki/Contemporary_classical_music "Contemporary classical music")' group in the world"[[8]](#cite_note-McCalla-8) and been praised in philosophical studies of music for the inclusiveness of its repertoire.[[9]](#cite_note-Ellis-9)

By the time the quartet celebrated its 25th anniversary in 1999, it had a repertoire of over 600 works, including 400 quartets written for it, more than 3,000 performances, seven first-prize [ASCAP](/wiki/ASCAP "ASCAP") awards, [Edison Awards](/wiki/Edison_Award "Edison Award") in classical and popular music, and had more than 1.5 million record sales.[[10]](#cite_note-Derk-10)

### 30th anniversary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kronos_Quartet&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 30th anniversary")]

When Kronos turned 30, in 2003, it decided to commission new pieces from composers under age 30, in hopes of encouraging talented young composers. The program, called the Under 30 Project, is now run in cooperation with [Carnegie Hall](/wiki/Carnegie_Hall "Carnegie Hall"), Cal Performances at the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"), and the [Montalvo Arts Center](/wiki/Montalvo_Arts_Center "Montalvo Arts Center"). The first recipient was [Alexandra du Bois](/wiki/Alexandra_du_Bois "Alexandra du Bois") (at the time a student at [Indiana University](/wiki/Indiana_University "Indiana University"), later a [Juilliard School](/wiki/Juilliard_School "Juilliard School") graduate),[[11]](#cite_note-Cahill-11) followed by Felipe Perez Santiago (born in Mexico in 1973),[[12]](#cite_note-Sisario-12) and Dan Visconti (born in Illinois in 1982);[[13]](#cite_note-Martini-13) in 2007, Israeli composer Aviya Kopelman became the fourth.[[14]](#cite_note-MusicalA-14)

### 40th anniversary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kronos_Quartet&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 40th anniversary")]

To celebrate its 40th year, the Kronos Quartet returned to Seattle, the city in which it first played, and worked in collaboration with Seattle's [Degenerate Art Ensemble](/wiki/Degenerate_Art_Ensemble "Degenerate Art Ensemble") to create a piece incorporating music, dance and video.[[2]](#cite_note-Graves-2) It celebrated its 40th anniversary with a sold-out performance at [Zellerbach Hall](/wiki/Zellerbach_Hall "Zellerbach Hall"), [UC Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"), in December 2013. The same year, [Michael Giacchino](/wiki/Michael_Giacchino "Michael Giacchino"), a soundtrack composer who often names his pieces with puns, published a piece named after Kronos, "The Kronos Wartet", as a part of the soundtrack to *[Star Trek Into Darkness](/wiki/Star_Trek_Into_Darkness "Star Trek Into Darkness")* for a scene that takes place on the fictional planet "Kronos".[[15]](#cite_note-15) (also spelled "[Qo'noS](/wiki/Qo%27noS "Qo'noS")").

## Members

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kronos_Quartet&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Members")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/timeline/7cqgm0lfiu4yzpxzor7zivh73gxfwe8.png)

## New music, contemporary classical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kronos_Quartet&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: New music, contemporary classical")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Laurie_Anderson_amidst_the_Kronos_Quartet_in_Chicago_after_performing_LANDFALL_2015-03-17_20.53.41_%2816851029595%29.jpg/250px-Laurie_Anderson_amidst_the_Kronos_Quartet_in_Chicago_after_performing_LANDFALL_2015-03-17_20.53.41_%2816851029595%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Laurie_Anderson_amidst_the_Kronos_Quartet_in_Chicago_after_performing_LANDFALL_2015-03-17_20.53.41_(16851029595).jpg)

On stage with [Laurie Anderson](/wiki/Laurie_Anderson "Laurie Anderson"), after performing *LANDFALL* at the [Harris Theater](/wiki/Harris_Theater_(Chicago) "Harris Theater (Chicago)") on March 17, 2015

Over 1,000 pieces have been created for the Kronos Quartet,[[16]](#cite_note-16) which has a long history of [commissioning](/wiki/Commission_(art) "Commission (art)") new works.[[17]](#cite_note-17) It has worked with many [minimalist](/wiki/Minimalist_music "Minimalist music") composers, including [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams_(composer) "John Adams (composer)"), [Arvo Pärt](/wiki/Arvo_P%C3%A4rt "Arvo Pärt"), [George Crumb](/wiki/George_Crumb "George Crumb"), [Henryk Górecki](/wiki/Henryk_G%C3%B3recki "Henryk Górecki"), [Steve Reich](/wiki/Steve_Reich "Steve Reich"), [John Luther Adams](/wiki/John_Luther_Adams "John Luther Adams"), [Roberto Paci Dalò](/wiki/Roberto_Paci_Dal%C3%B2 "Roberto Paci Dalò"), [Philip Glass](/wiki/Philip_Glass "Philip Glass"), [Terry Riley](/wiki/Terry_Riley "Terry Riley"), [Peter Sculthorpe](/wiki/Peter_Sculthorpe "Peter Sculthorpe") and [Kevin Volans](/wiki/Kevin_Volans "Kevin Volans"); collaborators hail from a diversity of countries—[Kaija Saariaho](/wiki/Kaija_Saariaho "Kaija Saariaho") from Finland, [Pēteris Vasks](/wiki/P%C4%93teris_Vasks "Pēteris Vasks") from Latvia, [Franghiz Ali-Zadeh](/wiki/Franghiz_Ali-Zadeh "Franghiz Ali-Zadeh") from Azerbaijan, [Homayun Sakhi](/wiki/Rainbow:_Music_of_Central_Asia_Vol._8 "Rainbow: Music of Central Asia Vol. 8") from Afghanistan, [Hamza El Din](/wiki/Hamza_El_Din "Hamza El Din") from [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), [Victoria Vita Polevá](/wiki/Victoria_Poleva "Victoria Poleva") from Ukraine and [Fernando Otero](/wiki/Fernando_Otero "Fernando Otero"),[[18]](#cite_note-18) [Astor Piazzolla](/wiki/Astor_Piazzolla "Astor Piazzolla"),[[19]](#cite_note-19) and [Osvaldo Golijov](/wiki/Osvaldo_Golijov "Osvaldo Golijov") from Argentina. Some of Kronos's string-quartet arrangements were published in 2007.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Diverse genres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kronos_Quartet&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Diverse genres")]

> I've always wanted the string quartet to be vital, and energetic, and alive, and cool, and not afraid to kick ass and be absolutely beautiful and ugly if it has to be. But it has to be expressive of life. To tell the story with grace and humor and depth. And to tell the whole story, if possible.

—David Harrington[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Kronos covers a very broad range of [musical genres](/wiki/Musical_genre "Musical genre"): [Mexican folk](/wiki/Music_of_Mexico "Music of Mexico"), [experimental](/wiki/Experimental_music "Experimental music"), pre-classical [early music](/wiki/Early_music "Early music"), movie soundtracks (*[Requiem for a Dream](/wiki/Requiem_for_a_Dream_(soundtrack) "Requiem for a Dream (soundtrack)")*, *[Heat](/wiki/Heat_(1995_film) "Heat (1995 film)")*, *[The Fountain](/wiki/The_Fountain_(film) "The Fountain (film)")*), [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") and [tango](/wiki/Tango_music "Tango music"). Kronos has also recorded adaptations of [Jimi Hendrix](/wiki/Jimi_Hendrix "Jimi Hendrix")'s "[Purple Haze](/wiki/Purple_Haze "Purple Haze")", [Sigur Rós](/wiki/Sigur_R%C3%B3s "Sigur Rós")'s "Flugufrelsarinn", [Television](/wiki/Television_(band) "Television (band)")'s "[Marquee Moon](/wiki/Marquee_Moon_(song) "Marquee Moon (song)")", [Raymond Scott](/wiki/Raymond_Scott "Raymond Scott")'s "Dinner Music for a Pack of Hungry Cannibals", and [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan")'s "[Don't Think Twice, It's All Right](/wiki/Don%27t_Think_Twice,_It%27s_All_Right "Don't Think Twice, It's All Right")".

The quartet has also worked with a variety of global musicians, including [Bollywood](/wiki/Bollywood "Bollywood") playback singer [Asha Bhosle](/wiki/Asha_Bhosle "Asha Bhosle");[[22]](#cite_note-22) Mexican-American painter [Gronk](/wiki/Gronk_(artist) "Gronk (artist)"); American [soprano](/wiki/Soprano "Soprano") [Dawn Upshaw](/wiki/Dawn_Upshaw "Dawn Upshaw"); [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") composer/performer [Pat Metheny](/wiki/Pat_Metheny "Pat Metheny"); Mexican rockers [Café Tacuba](/wiki/Caf%C3%A9_Tacuba "Café Tacuba"); [Azerbaijani](/wiki/Azerbaijani_people "Azerbaijani people") [mugam](/wiki/Mugam "Mugam") singer [Alim Qasimov](/wiki/Alim_Qasimov "Alim Qasimov"); and the Romanian [gypsy](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") band [Taraf de Haïdouks](/wiki/Taraf_de_Ha%C3%AFdouks "Taraf de Haïdouks") among others.

Kronos has performed live with the poet [Allen Ginsberg](/wiki/Allen_Ginsberg "Allen Ginsberg"), [Astor Piazzolla](/wiki/Astor_Piazzolla "Astor Piazzolla"), [The National](/wiki/The_National_(band) "The National (band)"), the [Modern Jazz Quartet](/wiki/Modern_Jazz_Quartet "Modern Jazz Quartet"), [Tom Waits](/wiki/Tom_Waits "Tom Waits"), [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie"), [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney") and [Björk](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rk "Björk"), and has recorded with [Nelly Furtado](/wiki/Nelly_Furtado "Nelly Furtado"), [Rokia Traoré](/wiki/Rokia_Traor%C3%A9 "Rokia Traoré"), [Joan Armatrading](/wiki/Joan_Armatrading "Joan Armatrading"), Brazilian [electronica](/wiki/Electronica "Electronica") artist [Amon Tobin](/wiki/Amon_Tobin "Amon Tobin"), Texas [yodeler](/wiki/Yodeler "Yodeler") [Don Walser](/wiki/Don_Walser "Don Walser"), [Faith No More](/wiki/Faith_No_More "Faith No More"), [Tiger Lillies](/wiki/Tiger_Lillies "Tiger Lillies") and [David Grisman](/wiki/David_Grisman "David Grisman").

In 1984, [Frank Zappa](/wiki/Frank_Zappa "Frank Zappa") wrote "None of the Above" for Kronos, of which it performs the first movement in the 2020 documentary *Zappa*, directed by Alex Winter. Kronos's artistic director, founder, and violinist David Harrington is also interviewed in the film.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

On the 1998 [Dave Matthews Band](/wiki/Dave_Matthews_Band "Dave Matthews Band") album *[Before These Crowded Streets](/wiki/Before_These_Crowded_Streets "Before These Crowded Streets")*, Kronos Quartet performed on the tracks "Halloween" and "The Stone". It also recorded for the 2007 [Nine Inch Nails](/wiki/Nine_Inch_Nails "Nine Inch Nails") remix album, *[Year Zero Remixed](/wiki/Year_Zero_Remixed "Year Zero Remixed")* doing a rendition of the track "Another Version of the Truth"". The group performed [Lee Brooks](/wiki/Lee_Brooks "Lee Brooks")'s score for the short film *[2081](/wiki/2081_(film) "2081 (film)")*, based on the [Kurt Vonnegut](/wiki/Kurt_Vonnegut "Kurt Vonnegut") short story "[Harrison Bergeron](/wiki/Harrison_Bergeron "Harrison Bergeron")".

In 2009, Kronos contributed an acoustic version of [Blind Willie Johnson](/wiki/Blind_Willie_Johnson "Blind Willie Johnson")'s "Dark Was the Night" for the AIDS benefit album *[Dark Was the Night](/wiki/Dark_Was_the_Night_(album) "Dark Was the Night (album)")* produced by the [Red Hot Organization](/wiki/Red_Hot_Organization "Red Hot Organization").

In 2017, the quartet performed as featured artists on the songs "Lost Light" and "Journey" on the soundtrack to the videogame *[Destiny 2](/wiki/Destiny_2 "Destiny 2")*.
